content.plain_english: This week your child is weighing things in kilograms (kg) and grams (g), measuring liquids in litres and millilitres, and reading temperatures in degrees Celsius. They are also learning to choose the right tool — scales, jug or thermometer — for the job.
content.in_school: Children weigh classroom objects on balance scales and pan scales, fill jugs with water to the marked lines, and read temperatures from real and drawn thermometers. They record results with units (e.g. 200 g, 500 ml, 18°C) and compare using <, > and =.
content.home_activity: Next time you bake or cook from a recipe, hand the kitchen scales to your child. Ask them to weigh out 200 g of flour, then 50 g of sugar. Ask which is heavier and how they know. If you have a measuring jug, end by pouring 250 ml of water and asking how much more is needed to make half a litre.
dinner_table_questions: Which is heavier — a bag of sugar or a tub of butter? How could we check?
How many millilitres of water do you think this glass holds?
What temperature do you think the fridge is in degrees Celsius?

key_vocabulary.definition: How heavy something is, measured in grams (g) or kilograms (kg). 1 kg is 1000 g.
How much liquid a container can hold, measured in millilitres (ml) or litres (l). 1 l is 1000 ml.
How hot or cold something is, measured in degrees Celsius (°C).
A tool used to measure mass. Some show a needle on a dial; others show a number on a screen.
A tool used to measure temperature.

example_questions: What unit would you use to weigh a banana — grams or kilograms?
A jug holds 750 ml. How much more water is needed to make 1 litre?
It is 4°C in the morning and 19°C at lunchtime. How much warmer has it become?
content.times_table_tip: 1 kg is 1000 g and 1 litre is 1000 ml. Counting in 100s up to 1000 — and back again — helps your child move between the units quickly.
